questions.question: Explain the difference between signed and unsigned integers in C++. Give an example of when each would be used.
keyPoints: Signed integers can store negative and positive values using 2's complement representation
Unsigned integers store only non-negative values (0 and positive)
Signed int range: -2^(n-1) to 2^(n-1)-1
Unsigned int range: 0 to 2^n-1
Unsigned used for bit manipulation, array indexing, representing quantities that cannot be negative

questions.question: What is type conversion in C++? Differentiate between implicit and explicit type conversion with examples.
keyPoints: Type conversion transforms one data type to another
Implicit conversion (coercion) is automatic by compiler - e.g., int to float
Explicit conversion uses cast operators - e.g., (int)3.14 or static_cast<int>(3.14)
Implicit may cause data loss when converting float to int
Explicit gives programmer control over conversion

questions.question: Write a C++ program to demonstrate the difference between pre-increment (++i) and post-increment (i++).
keyPoints: Pre-increment increments then returns new value
Post-increment returns current value then increments
Demonstrate with cout statements showing output
Show that in expressions, pre-increment is faster
Include both in assignment and standalone usage

questions.question: What are the different storage classes in C++? Explain the difference between auto and static storage classes.
keyPoints: Four storage classes: auto, register, static, extern
auto: automatic local variable, destroyed after scope ends
static: retains value between function calls, lifetime is entire program
register: requests CPU register storage for faster access
extern: external linkage for variables defined in other files

questions.question: Explain the difference between = and == operators in C++. Why is it important to understand this difference?
keyPoints: = is assignment operator - assigns value to variable
== is comparison/relational operator - checks equality
Using = instead of == in conditions is a common bug
This causes assignment instead of comparison (x=5 assigns 5, returns 5 which is true)
Always use == for comparison, some use Yoda conditions (5==x) to catch typo

questions.question: What are logical operators in C++? Evaluate the expression: (5>3 && 2<1 || !(3==3))
keyPoints: Three logical operators: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)
&& returns true only if both operands are true
|| returns true if at least one operand is true
! inverts boolean value
Step-by-step: (true && false || !true) = (false || false) = false

questions.question: What is the sizeof operator? Write a program to find the size of int, float, double, and char on your system.
keyPoints: sizeof returns size in bytes of type or variable
Usage: sizeof(int) or sizeof(x)
Size varies by system and compiler
Useful for portable code and memory calculations
Can be used with primitive and user-defined types

questions.question: Explain all arithmetic operators in C++ with examples. Also explain operator precedence and how parentheses affect evaluation.
keyPoints: Arithmetic: + (add), - (subtract), * (multiply), / (divide), % (modulo)
/ and % behave differently for integers vs floats
Precedence: * / % before + -
Left-to-right associativity for same precedence
Parentheses override default precedence
Use examples showing each operator with sample outputs

questions.question: Write a C++ program that demonstrates the use of various operators: arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, and assignment operators in a single program.
keyPoints: Include all arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %)
Include relational operators (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=)
Include logical operators (&&, ||, !)
Include bitwise operators (&, |, ^, <<, >>)
Include assignment operators (=, +=, -=, *=, /=, %=)
Use appropriate variable types and demonstrate outputs

questions.question: What is the difference between const and constexpr in C++? When would you use each? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: const: runtime constant, value fixed after initialization
constexpr: compile-time constant, evaluated during compilation
const can be used with runtime values (function parameters)
constexpr must have value known at compile time
constexpr enables performance optimization by avoiding runtime computation
Use const for function arguments, constexpr for array sizes, mathematical constants

questions.question: Write a C++ program to create a calculator using switch statement that performs arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /, %) using variables and operators.
keyPoints: Accept two numbers from user
Use switch statement for operation selection
Handle all 5 arithmetic operations
Include input validation (check for division by zero)
Display appropriate results
Use proper variable types and clear output
